Risk Description: Angular applications are susceptible to XSS attacks when user-generated content is rendered without proper sanitization. Despite Angular's built-in DomSanitizer, developers may bypass security contexts using methods like bypassSecurityTrustHtml, bypassSecurityTrustScript, or bypassSecurityTrustUrl, allowing malicious scripts to execute in the browser. Additionally, improper handling of innerHTML, dynamic template compilation, or third-party library integration can introduce XSS vulnerabilities.
Potential Impact: Successful XSS exploitation can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, unauthorized actions on behalf of users, data exfiltration, malware distribution, defacement of the application, loss of customer trust, regulatory fines under GDPR/CCPA, reputational damage, and potential legal liability. In enterprise environments, this can compromise sensitive business data and intellectual property.

Risk Description: Angular applications that interact with backend APIs without proper CSRF protection are vulnerable to CSRF attacks. While Angular's HttpClient includes built-in CSRF protection using tokens (XSRF-TOKEN), misconfigurations, disabled protection, or improper token validation on the backend can expose the application. Attackers can trick authenticated users into executing unwanted actions, such as changing account settings, initiating transactions, or modifying data.
Potential Impact: CSRF attacks can result in unauthorized state-changing operations, financial fraud, data manipulation, compromised user accounts, regulatory non-compliance, loss of customer confidence, legal consequences, and significant remediation costs. In financial or healthcare applications, this can lead to severe business disruption and regulatory penalties.

Risk Description: Angular applications that expose object references (e.g., user IDs, document IDs) in URLs, API calls, or route parameters without proper authorization checks are vulnerable to IDOR attacks. Attackers can manipulate these references to access unauthorized resources, view sensitive data belonging to other users, or perform unauthorized operations. This is particularly critical in applications with role-based access control (RBAC) or multi-tenant architectures.
Potential Impact: IDOR vulnerabilities can lead to unauthorized data access, privacy violations, data breaches, regulatory fines under GDPR/HIPAA/PCI-DSS, loss of competitive advantage, reputational damage, customer churn, and potential lawsuits. In enterprise environments, this can expose confidential business information and intellectual property.
